core_takeaway: Agent_1 opened with Stone/Jewel ambitions but the consensus allocation gave Agent_1 only Quill while giving Agent_5 high-value Pencil, so first position did not translate into payoff advantage.
why_or_counterexample: Counterexample: Agent_1 helped create the final deal, but that deal ceded its top items to stronger claimants and explicitly satisfied Agent_5's Pencil demand; all proposals were identical and unanimously accepted.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Agent_1 began by targeting Stone and Jewel, but public discussion shifted toward protecting Agent_2 on Jewel, moving Stone to Agent_3, and leaving or allocating Pencil for Agent_5.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Five private_thinking entries are present. They converge on Option C or a priority-holder allocation in which Agent_1 does not retain Stone or Jewel, while Agent_5 keeps pursuing Quill/Pencil access.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Five formal proposals are present and identical: Agent_1 gets Quill, Agent_2 gets Jewel, Agent_3 gets Stone, Agent_4 gets Apple, and Agent_5 gets Pencil.
phase_notes.private_voting: Twenty-five private votes are present and all accept. Voters repeatedly cite identical allocation, immediate top-item payoff, and momentum rather than any first-speaker advantage.
phase_notes.reflection: No reflection section appears in the transcript; reflection_entries metadata is 0.

mechanisms.theme: Agent_1 ceded its original top-item agenda
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1's initial ask centered on Stone and Jewel, but its own formal proposal sent Stone to Agent_3 and Jewel to Agent_2, leaving Agent_1 with Quill. The early position supplied framing, not payoff protection.

evidence.quote: My top priorities: Stone (45) first, Jewel (36) second.

mechanisms.theme: Agent_5 converted last-slot advocacy into Pencil
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_5 repeatedly named Quill and Pencil as its core targets and pushed for immediate Pencil access. The accepted allocation gave Agent_5 Pencil, worth 43 to Agent_5, which was enough to beat Agent_1's 17-point Quill outcome.

evidence.quote: my top values are Quill (56) and Pencil (43)

mechanisms.explanation: The final deal was justified as matching items to agents who valued or defended them most. That logic favored Agent_2 on Jewel, Agent_3 on Stone, Agent_4 on Apple, and Agent_5 on Pencil, while Agent_1 received a secondary item.

evidence.quote: Allocates each item to a high-priority holder
Allocates top-valued items to corresponding agents

mechanisms.theme: Private convergence locked in Agent_1's lower-payoff role
mechanisms.explanation: The private_thinking section shows broad convergence on Option C or a similar allocation before formal proposals. Even Agent_1's own private plan defaulted to Stone for Agent_3, Quill for Agent_1, and Jewel for Agent_2, so there was no hidden first-position leverage to reclaim the high-value items.

evidence.quote: Default Step 1 plan: adopt Option C
Step 1 consensus across agents tends to favor a path
The aim is to secure Quill and Pencil by Round 2

mechanisms.theme: Identical proposals and unanimous voting neutralized proposer-order effects
mechanisms.explanation: By voting time every formal proposal had the same allocation. Proposal #5 was the accepted one, but the tabulation shows all five would have passed 5-0, so the outcome reflects settled consensus rather than a benefit from moving first.

evidence.quote: Identical allocation as the other proposals
